clues.text: A newborn girl is evaluated for poor feeding, generalized hypotonia, and intermittent cyanosis during feeds
The pregnancy occurs when the mother was 39 years old and prenatal serum screening demonstrated elevated beta-hCG with decreased AFP
Physical exam reveals a flat facial profile and a single transverse palmar crease
Echocardiography demonstrates an AVSD
Genetic counseling describes an increased risk for early-onset Alzheimer's disease and acute lymphoblastic leukemia later in life

clues.detail: Karyotype analysis demonstrates trisomy of chromosome 21

1.description: Down Syndrome is the most common survivable autosomal trisomy and is usually caused by meiotic nondisjunction, with risk increasing with advanced maternal age. It classically presents with hypotonia, developmental delay, upslanting palpebral fissures, and a single transverse palmar crease, often with congenital heart disease such as an atrioventricular septal defect. Prenatal screening may show increased nuchal translucency, elevated β-hCG and inhibin A, and decreased AFP. Diagnosis is confirmed with karyotyping. Patients are at increased risk for leukemia, hypothyroidism, and early-onset Alzheimer disease.
1.management: Early developmental intervention, surveillance for associated complications, surgical correction of congenital anomalies, and family genetic counseling.

clues.text: A 14 year old girl is evaluated for short stature and delayed pubertal development
Further history reveals that she has never menstruated and describes difficulty "keeping up" with peers during physical activity
Physical exam reveals a broad chest with widely spaced nipples and webbed neck
Laboratory evaluation reveals elevated FSH and LH levels

clues.detail: CXR demonstrates focal indentation of the distal aortic arch consistent with coarctation of the aorta. "Figure of 3" sign
Karyotype analysis demonstrates complete loss of an X chromosome

1.description: Turner Syndrome is a chromosomal disorder caused by complete or partial absence of one X chromosome, classically presenting with short stature, primary amenorrhea, webbed neck, and broad chest with widely spaced nipples. Patients commonly develop streak ovaries leading to hypergonadotropic hypogonadism and infertility. Associated congenital abnormalities include coarctation of the aorta and bicuspid aortic valve. Diagnosis is confirmed with karyotyping demonstrating monosomy X (45,X). Common findings include lymphedema, horseshoe kidney, and delayed puberty.
1.management: Growth hormone therapy, estrogen replacement, cardiovascular surveillance, fertility counseling, and monitoring for endocrine, renal, and cardiovascular complications throughout life.

clues.text: A 17 year old boy is evaluated for delayed secondary sexual development and difficulty gaining muscle mass despite regular training
He reports worsening fatigue in school and has struggled with language-based learning difficulties since childhood
Physical examination reveals tall stature with disproportionately long extremities, sparse facial hair, small firm testes, and gynecomastia
Semen analysis demonstrates azoospermia, and bone density imaging reveals decreased bone mineral density
Laboratory studies reveal elevated FSH and LH levels with low serum testosterone consistent with primary testicular failure.

clues.detail: Karyotype analysis demonstrates an additional X chromosome

1.description: Klinefelter Syndrome is a chromosomal disorder caused by the presence of an additional X chromosome in males, most commonly due to meiotic nondisjunction. It classically presents with tall stature, small firm testes, gynecomastia, infertility, and delayed secondary sexual development. Patients often develop hypergonadotropic hypogonadism with low testosterone and elevated FSH and LH levels. Associated findings include learning difficulties, decreased bone density, and azoospermia. Diagnosis is confirmed with karyotyping demonstrating a 47,XXY genotype.
1.management: Testosterone replacement therapy, fertility counseling, psychosocial support, and monitoring for osteoporosis, metabolic disease, and breast cancer risk

clues.text: A 29 year old woman at 10 weeks' gestation presents with persistent nausea, vomiting, and inability to tolerate food and fluids for the past 2 weeks
She has lost 6 kg since becoming pregnant and reports dizziness when standing. This is her first pregnancy
Physical exam reveals dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, and orthostatic hypotension
Urinalysis demonstrates ketonuria and obstetric US confirms viable intrauterine pregnancy without multiple gestation
Lab studies reveal hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis
She is hospitalized and given IV thiamine before D5W

1.description: Hyperemesis Gravidarum is a severe form of nausea and vomiting during pregnancy that results in dehydration, weight loss exceeding 5% of prepregnancy body weight, ketonuria, and electrolyte abnormalities. It typically occurs during the first trimester and is associated with elevated β-hCG levels, multiple gestation, and molar pregnancy. Patients commonly develop hypokalemic, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis due to prolonged vomiting. Diagnosis is clinical after excluding alternative causes.
1.management: Intravenous fluids, antiemetics, electrolyte replacement, nutritional support, and administration of thiamine before dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.

clues.text: A 28 year old woman at 11 weeks' gestation presents with persistent nausea and vomiting and intermittent vaginal bleeding
She reports symptoms have been progressively worsening and her uterine size feels bigger than expected for gestational age
Physical examination reveals a uterus consistent with a 16-week gestation despite dating of 11 weeks, with no fetal heart tones detected
Serum β-hCG levels are 250,000 mIU/mL

clues.detail: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates a heterogeneous intrauterine mass with numerous small cystic spaces and no identifiable fetus. "Snowstorm" or "bunch of grapes" sign

clues.text: Karyotyping reveals a 46,XX conception derived entirely from paternal genetic material.

1.description: Hydatidiform Mole is a form of gestational trophoblastic disease characterized by abnormal proliferation of trophoblastic tissue and hydropic swelling of chorionic villi. Complete moles typically result from fertilization of an enucleated ovum by one sperm that duplicates its chromosomes, producing a 46,XX paternal genome with no fetal tissue. Patients commonly present in the first trimester with vaginal bleeding, severe nausea and vomiting, uterine size greater than expected for gestational age, and markedly elevated β-hCG levels. Ultrasound classically demonstrates a “snowstorm” appearance.
1.management: Uterine evacuation, serial β-hCG monitoring, and surveillance for gestational trophoblastic neoplasia

clues.text: A 31 year old woman presents with persistent vaginal bleeding and progressive shortness of breath three months after a pregnancy ended.
She reports that the pregnancy was complicated by a molar gestation requiring uterine evacuation
Physical examination reveals tachycardia and scattered bilateral pulmonary crackles.
Serum β-hCG is markedly elevated, and biopsy demonstrates malignant proliferation of cytotrophoblasts and syncytiotrophoblasts without chorionic villi.

clues.detail: Axial CT demonstrates well circumscribed, rounded soft tissue pulmonary masses known as cannonball metastases

clues.text: Despite widespread metastatic disease, the tumor demonstrates an excellent response to chemotherapy.

1.description: Choriocarcinoma is a highly malignant trophoblastic tumor that may arise after a hydatidiform mole, spontaneous abortion, ectopic pregnancy, or normal pregnancy. It consists of malignant cytotrophoblasts and syncytiotrophoblasts without chorionic villi and produces markedly elevated β-hCG levels. Patients commonly present with persistent vaginal bleeding and symptoms related to hematogenous metastases, most frequently involving the lungs and brain. Diagnosis is based on elevated β-hCG and imaging demonstrating metastatic disease. Pathognomonic pathology shows trophoblastic proliferation without villous structures.
1.management: Chemotherapy, often with excellent cure rates even in the presence of widespread metastases.

clues.text: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with RLQ pain and light vaginal bleeding after missing her last menstrual period.
She has a history of pelvic inflammatory disease and was treated for chlamydia several years ago
Physical examination reveals left adnexal tenderness and cervical motion tenderness
Serial β-hCG measurements increase more slowly than expected for a viable intrauterine pregnancy
US shows an empty endometrial cavity and complex extra-adnexal mass
She suddenly develops severe abdominal pain, hypotension, and a positive FAST examination demonstrating free intraperitoneal fluid

1.description: Ectopic Pregnancy occurs when a fertilized ovum implants outside the uterine cavity, most commonly within the fallopian tube. Major risk factors include pelvic inflammatory disease, prior ectopic pregnancy, tubal surgery, infertility treatments, and smoking. Patients classically present with the triad of amenorrhea, abdominal pain, and vaginal bleeding. Diagnosis is made using serial β-hCG measurements and transvaginal ultrasound demonstrating the absence of an intrauterine pregnancy when expected. Pathognomonic findings include visualization of an extrauterine gestational sac with a yolk sac or embryo.
1.management: Management depends on stability and includes methotrexate or surgical intervention, with ruptured ectopic pregnancy requiring emergency surgery

1.diagnosis: Polyendocrine Metabolic Ovarian Syndrome (PMOS; formerly PCOS)

clues.text: A 23-year-old woman presents with irregular menstrual cycles and difficulty conceiving after one year of unprotected intercourse
She reports gradual weight gain since adolescence and has a family history of type 2 diabetes
Physical examination reveals central obesity, acne, and coarse hair growth on the chin and upper lip
Laboratory studies reveal elevated testosterone levels, an increased LH:FSH ratio, and evidence of insulin resistance

clues.detail: Ovarian ultrasound reveals 8-10 peripherally arranged follicles in the ovary. "String of pearls"

clues.text: Endometrial biopsy performed for abnormal uterine bleeding demonstrates endometrial hyperplasia due to chronic unopposed estrogen exposure

1.description: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome is a common endocrine disorder characterized by hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovarian morphology. Insulin resistance plays a central role, increasing ovarian androgen production and contributing to anovulation. Patients commonly present with irregular menses, infertility, hirsutism, acne, obesity, and metabolic syndrome. Diagnosis is typically based on the Rotterdam criteria, requiring two of three findings: hyperandrogenism, oligo/anovulation, or polycystic ovaries on ultrasound. Characteristic imaging shows multiple peripheral ovarian follicles (“string of pearls”). Long-term risks include type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and endometrial hyperplasia or carcinoma.
1.management: Includes weight loss, combined hormonal contraceptives, metformin, and ovulation induction for fertility

clues.text: A 34-year-old pregnant woman at 32 weeks’ gestation is referred to a high-risk obstetrics clinic after routine ultrasound identifies an abnormal placental implantation
She has had three prior cesarean deliveries and was diagnosed with placenta previa earlier in the pregnancy
She remains asymptomatic but is counseled regarding a high risk of severe hemorrhage at the time of delivery
Targeted obstetric ultrasound demonstrates multiple placental lacunae, loss of the normal hypoechoic retroplacental zone, and increased vascularity extending toward the myometrium
At delivery, the placenta fails to separate normally because chorionic villi are directly attached to and invading the uterine wall due to defective decidualization
A planned cesarean hysterectomy is performed with the placenta intentionally left in situ to prevent life-threatening hemorrhage

1.description: Placenta Accreta Spectrum is a disorder of abnormal placental implantation in which chorionic villi attach to or invade the myometrium due to defective decidualization. The spectrum includes placenta accreta (attachment to myometrium), increta (invasion into myometrium), and percreta (penetration through the uterus). Major risk factors include placenta previa and prior cesarean delivery, with risk increasing as the number of cesarean sections rises. Diagnosis is usually made prenatally by ultrasound demonstrating placental lacunae and loss of the retroplacental clear zone.
1.management: Planned cesarean hysterectomy with the placenta left in situ because attempted manual removal can cause massive obstetric hemorrhage. TXA is also used to help control the bleed.

clues.text: A 28-year-old woman pregnant with twins at 22 weeks’ gestation presents for routine prenatal assessment after noticing rapid abdominal enlargement and increasing shortness of breath over the past several weeks
First-trimester ultrasound confirmed a monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy
Fundal height measures significantly greater than expected for gestational age, and the pregnancy has become increasingly uncomfortable due to uterine distension
Ultrasound demonstrates one twin with severe oligohydramnios appearing fixed against the uterine wall, while the co-twin has marked polyhydramnios and a distended bladder
Placental examination reveals vascular anastomoses that allow chronic net blood flow from one twin to the other
The donor twin develops growth restriction and anemia, while the recipient twin develops volume overload and is at risk for high-output cardiac failure

1.description: Twin-to-Twin Transfusion Syndrome is a complication of monochorionic twin pregnancies caused by unbalanced placental vascular anastomoses that result in chronic blood flow from a donor twin to a recipient twin. The donor twin develops hypovolemia, growth restriction, oligohydramnios, and anemia, whereas the recipient twin develops hypervolemia, polyhydramnios, cardiomegaly, and risk of high-output heart failure. Diagnosis is made by ultrasound demonstrating the classic oligohydramnios-polyhydramnios sequence and the “stuck twin” phenomenon.
1.management: Management depends on severity and gestational age, with fetoscopic laser ablation of placental vascular anastomoses representing the definitive treatment for severe disease

clues.text: A newborn boy is noted to have weakness of both lower extremities and difficulty moving his feet shortly after delivery
The mother received limited prenatal care and did not take folic acid supplementation before conception
Physical examination reveals a lumbosacral midline defect covered by a thin membranous sac, with decreased sensation and reflexes in the lower limbs
Maternal prenatal screening showed markedly elevated alpha-fetoprotein levels, and embryologic review reveals failure of caudal neuropore closure during the fourth week of development

clues.detail: Sagittal T2-weighted MRI shows sac-like posterior herniation of the dural sac through the posterior neural arch defect containing fat, meninges, and tethered cord consistent with lipomyelomeningocele

clues.text: Brain MRI demonstrates downward displacement of the cerebellar tonsils and vermis through the foramen magnum with associated hydrocephalus

1.description: Spina Bifida is a neural tube defect caused by failure of caudal neuropore closure during early embryonic development, resulting in incomplete fusion of the vertebral arches. Risk factors include folate deficiency, maternal diabetes, obesity, and exposure to folate-antagonist medications such as valproate. Myelomeningocele, the most severe common form, presents with protrusion of meninges and neural tissue, lower extremity weakness, sensory deficits, and bowel or bladder dysfunction. Maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein is typically elevated, and prenatal ultrasound may detect the defect.
1.management: Folic acid prevention via supplementation, surgical repair, management of neurologic complications, and treatment of associated Chiari II malformation and hydrocephalus

clues.text: A 31 year old mother presents for a routine prenatal ultrasound at 18 weeks’ gestation that reveals severe abnormalities of the fetal head and central nervous system
The mother did not receive folic acid supplementation prior to conception and has a history of poorly controlled diabetes
At delivery, the neonate is noted to have absence of the cranial vault with exposed disorganized neural tissue above the orbits
Embryologic review demonstrates failure of rostral neuropore closure during the fourth week of development, resulting in degeneration of exposed neural tissue
Prenatal ultrasound demonstrates absence of the calvarium and cerebral hemispheres with preservation of facial structures
Maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein and amniotic fluid acetylcholinesterase levels are markedly elevated

1.description: Anencephaly is a lethal neural tube defect caused by failure of rostral neuropore closure during the fourth week of embryonic development. This results in absence of the calvarium and most cerebral hemispheres, with exposed neural tissue that subsequently degenerates. Major risk factors include folate deficiency, maternal diabetes, obesity, and folate-antagonist medications. Maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein and amniotic fluid acetylcholinesterase levels are markedly elevated. Prenatal ultrasound classically demonstrates absence of the cranial vault and cerebral structures. Diagnosis is usually made antenatally.
1.management: There is no curative treatment, and management focuses on prenatal counseling, supportive care, and prevention through preconception folic acid supplementation

clues.text: An 8-year-old boy is referred for evaluation of short stature and poor growth despite otherwise normal nutrition and overall health
His father and paternal grandfather were both described as unusually short and required cardiac follow-up during childhood
Physical examination reveals hypertelorism, low-set ears, a webbed neck, and a broad chest with widely spaced nipples
Echocardiography demonstrates pulmonary valve stenosis with right ventricular hypertrophy
Genetic testing identifies a pathogenic mutation affecting the RAS/MAPK signaling pathway.
Chromosomal analysis demonstrates a normal male karyotype

1.description: Noonan Syndrome is an autosomal dominant developmental disorder most commonly caused by mutations in genes of the RAS/MAPK signaling pathway, particularly PTPN11. It classically presents with short stature, hypertelorism, low-set ears, webbed neck, and congenital heart disease, most commonly pulmonary valve stenosis or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. The syndrome can affect both males and females and may resemble Turner syndrome despite a normal karyotype. Additional features include developmental delay, cryptorchidism, and bleeding abnormalities. Diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing.
1.management: Cardiac surveillance, developmental support, treatment of growth and endocrine issues, and monitoring for associated complications throughout life.

clues.text: An 8-month-old infant is brought to clinic because of progressive loss of previously acquired developmental milestones and increasing difficulty sitting unsupported
The child was developing normally until 6 months of age, and both parents are of Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry
Neurologic examination reveals hypotonia, exaggerated startle response to sound, and progressive visual impairment
Enzyme testing demonstrates markedly reduced hexosaminidase A activity, resulting in accumulation of GM2 gangliosides within lysosomes
Brain MRI demonstrates progressive cerebral atrophy without evidence of structural malformations
Fundoscopic examination reveals a cherry-red macula without hepatosplenomegaly

1.description: Tay–Sachs Disease is an autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disorder caused by deficiency of hexosaminidase A, leading to accumulation of GM2 gangliosides within neurons. It classically presents in infancy with developmental regression, hypotonia, exaggerated startle response, progressive neurodegeneration, blindness, and seizures. The disease is particularly prevalent among individuals of Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry. A cherry-red macula is a characteristic finding, while hepatosplenomegaly is absent, helping distinguish it from Niemann–Pick disease. Diagnosis is confirmed by demonstrating reduced hexosaminidase A activity.
1.management: There is no curative treatment; management is supportive and focuses on symptom control, nutritional support, and family genetic counseling.

clues.text: A newborn boy is admitted to the neonatal intensive care unit because of feeding difficulties, cyanotic episodes, and failure to pass a feeding tube through the nasal cavity
Pregnancy and family history are unremarkable, and the infant appears to have multiple congenital anomalies affecting several organ systems
Echocardiography demonstrates tetralogy of Fallot, and CT imaging confirms bilateral choanal atresia
Ophthalmologic examination reveals a coloboma involving the retina and optic disc

clues.detail: Physical examination reveals malformed external ears, facial asymmetry, and absent response to auditory stimuli

clues.text: Genetic testing identifies a pathogenic mutation in the CHD7 gene, which regulates embryologic development of multiple organ systems

1.description: CHARGE Syndrome is a rare autosomal dominant developmental disorder caused by mutations in the CHD7 gene. It is characterized by multiple congenital anomalies including coloboma, choanal atresia, congenital heart defects, growth and developmental delay, genital abnormalities, and ear malformations with hearing loss. Most cases occur sporadically due to de novo mutations. Diagnosis is based on clinical features and confirmed with genetic testing. Common cardiac abnormalities include tetralogy of Fallot and atrioventricular septal defects.
1.management: Multidisciplinary care involving cardiology, otolaryngology, ophthalmology, audiology, developmental services, and surgical correction of life-threatening anomalies such as choanal atresia

clues.text: A 17-year-old boy presents after his family physician notices a new heart murmur during a routine sports physical. He reports occasional shortness of breath during exercise
His father died suddenly at 38 years of age from an unexplained cardiovascular event
Physical examination reveals tall stature, disproportionately long extremities, arachnodactyly, and hypermobile joints
Echocardiography demonstrates dilation of the aortic root with associated aortic regurgitation
Genetic testing identifies a mutation in the FBN1 gene, resulting in abnormal fibrillin-1 and dysregulated transforming growth factor-β signaling
Slit-lamp examination demonstrates bilateral upward displacement of the crystalline lenses

1.description: Marfan Syndrome is an autosomal dominant connective tissue disorder caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene, leading to abnormal fibrillin-1 and excessive transforming growth factor-β signaling. Patients classically present with tall stature, arachnodactyly, pectus deformities, joint hypermobility, and ectopia lentis. The most serious complication is progressive aortic root dilation, which may lead to aortic aneurysm, dissection, or sudden death. Diagnosis is based on clinical findings, family history, imaging, and genetic testing.
1.management: Regular cardiovascular surveillance, beta-blockers or angiotensin receptor blockers to slow aortic dilation, activity modification, and prophylactic surgical repair of the aorta when indicated.

clues.text: A 2-year-old boy is referred for evaluation of short stature after falling below the expected growth curve despite normal developmental milestones and overall health
Neither parent is affected, but the child was born to a 43-year-old father
Physical examination reveals short proximal limbs, frontal bossing, and a relatively large head with normal trunk length
Genetic testing identifies a gain-of-function mutation in the FGFR3 gene that inhibits endochondral ossification

clues.detail: Radiograph demonstrate normal density with marked shortening of tthe long bones

clues.text: Intelligence is normal, and the child develops lumbar spinal stenosis and recurrent otitis media during follow-up

1.description: Achondroplasia is the most common form of disproportionate short stature and is caused by an autosomal dominant gain-of-function mutation in the FGFR3 gene, which inhibits endochondral bone growth. Most cases occur sporadically and are associated with advanced paternal age. Patients classically present with rhizomelic limb shortening, frontal bossing, midface hypoplasia, and macrocephaly with normal intelligence. Characteristic radiographic findings include shortened long bones and narrowing of the interpedicular distance in the lumbar spine. Diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing.
1.management: Monitoring growth and development, treating complications such as spinal stenosis and recurrent ear infections, and providing orthopedic and supportive care

clues.text: A 7-year-old boy is referred for evaluation of learning difficulties, poor academic performance, and delayed language development
His maternal uncle had intellectual disability, and several male relatives on his mother's side required special education services
Physical examination reveals a long narrow face, prominent ears, and behavioral features including poor eye contact and repetitive hand movements.
Neuropsychological testing demonstrates intellectual disability with prominent deficits in language and executive functioning, while hearing and vision assessments are normal
During adolescence, he develops macroorchidism despite otherwise normal pubertal progression
Molecular testing reveals expansion of CGG trinucleotide repeats in the FMR1 gene, resulting in hypermethylation and reduced production of fragile X mental retardation protein (FMRP)

1.description: Fragile X Syndrome is the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability and is caused by CGG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene on the X chromosome. Expansion leads to hypermethylation and reduced production of FMRP, a protein important for normal synaptic development. Patients commonly present with developmental delay, intellectual disability, language impairment, autism spectrum features, a long narrow face, and prominent ears. Macroorchidism typically develops after puberty. Diagnosis is confirmed by molecular genetic testing.
1.management: Educational support, behavioral therapy, speech therapy, and treatment of associated psychiatric or developmental conditions. Genetic counseling is recommended because of the X-linked inheritance pattern.

clues.text: An 18-month-old girl is brought to clinic because she has stopped using words she previously knew and is no longer interested in interactive play
Pregnancy, birth history, and early developmental milestones were normal during the first year of life
Physical examination reveals loss of purposeful hand use and repetitive hand-wringing movements. Head circumference has fallen from the 75th to the 10th percentile over the past year
Brain MRI demonstrates generalized cerebral volume loss without focal structural abnormalities, and developmental assessment confirms severe impairment of language and social communication
Genetic testing identifies a pathogenic mutation in the MECP2 gene on the X chromosome, resulting in abnormal neuronal maturation and synaptic function
Over the next several years, she develops seizures, gait abnormalities, and episodic hyperventilation while remaining socially engaged with caregivers

1.description: Rett Syndrome is an X-linked dominant neurodevelopmental disorder caused by mutations in the MECP2 gene and occurs almost exclusively in females. Affected children typically develop normally during the first 6–18 months of life before experiencing developmental regression, loss of language and purposeful hand use, acquired microcephaly, and characteristic hand-wringing movements. Additional features include seizures, gait abnormalities, breathing irregularities, and intellectual disability. Diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing. Brain imaging may demonstrate nonspecific cerebral atrophy.
1.management: Physical therapy, speech therapy, seizure management, nutritional support, and multidisciplinary developmental care. Early recognition is important for intervention and family counseling.

clues.text: A 14-year-old boy is referred for evaluation of progressive drooping of both eyelids and worsening difficulty tracking moving objects over the past several years
He has experienced declining exercise tolerance and difficulty keeping up with peers, despite normal childhood development
Physical examination reveals bilateral ptosis, severe limitation of eye movements in all directions, and mild proximal muscle weakness
Fundoscopic examination reveals a progressive pigmentary retinopathy

clues.detail: 12-lead ECG demonstrates complete AV dissociation and right bundle branch block

clues.text: Muscle biopsy demonstrates ragged-red fibres and genetic testing identifies a large mitochondrial DNA deletion.

1.description: Kearns–Sayre Syndrome is a rare mitochondrial disorder caused by large deletions in mitochondrial DNA. It typically presents before age 20 with progressive external ophthalmoplegia, bilateral ptosis, and pigmentary retinopathy. Additional manifestations include proximal muscle weakness, hearing loss, cerebellar dysfunction, endocrinopathies, and potentially life-threatening cardiac conduction abnormalities such as complete heart block. Diagnosis is supported by muscle biopsy demonstrating ragged-red fibers and confirmed by mitochondrial genetic testing. Because mitochondrial DNA is maternally inherited, family counseling may be appropriate.
1.management: Cardiac monitoring, pacemaker placement when indicated, treatment of endocrine complications, rehabilitation, and multidisciplinary follow-up.

clues.text: An 8-month-old infant is referred for evaluation of global developmental delay and poor muscle tone. Parents report difficulty with feeding and delayed motor milestones
Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated, but the infant has experienced poor weight gain and recurrent feeding difficulties since birth
Physical examination reveals hypotonia, deep-set eyes, a flat nasal bridge, straight eyebrows, and a pointed chin
Echocardiography demonstrates dilated cardiomyopathy, and brain MRI reveals mild cerebral atrophy with ventriculomegaly
The child later develops seizures and moderate intellectual disability.
Chromosomal microarray identifies a terminal deletion involving the short arm of chromosome 1 at band p36

1.description: 1p36 Deletion Syndrome is one of the most common terminal deletion syndromes and results from deletion of genetic material on the short arm of chromosome 1. It classically presents with hypotonia, developmental delay, intellectual disability, feeding difficulties, seizures, and characteristic facial features including deep-set eyes, straight eyebrows, a flat nasal bridge, and a pointed chin. Associated abnormalities include hearing loss, vision impairment, congenital heart disease, and dilated cardiomyopathy. Diagnosis is confirmed by chromosomal microarray or genetic testing demonstrating a 1p36 deletion.
1.management: Developmental therapies, seizure management, cardiac surveillance, nutritional support, and multidisciplinary care

clues.text: A 2-month-old infant is referred for evaluation of poor weight gain and a newly detected heart murmur during a routine well-baby visit
The mother recalls a mild febrile illness with rash during the first trimester after returning from international travel and was unsure of her vaccination status
Physical examination reveals bilateral cloudy lenses and diminished response to auditory stimuli
Echocardiography demonstrates a patent ductus arteriosus with left-to-right shunting
The infant demonstrates sensorineural deafness, cataracts, and congenital heart disease
Serologic testing demonstrates rubella-specific IgM antibodies in the infant

1.description: Congenital Rubella Syndrome results from transplacental infection with rubella virus, particularly during the first trimester when fetal organogenesis is occurring. The classic triad consists of sensorineural hearing loss, cataracts, and congenital heart disease, most commonly patent ductus arteriosus. Additional manifestations include growth restriction, developmental delay, microcephaly, hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, and the characteristic “blueberry muffin” rash caused by extramedullary hematopoiesis. Diagnosis is confirmed by rubella-specific IgM antibodies or persistent rubella infection in the infant.
1.management: Supportive and multidisciplinary, focusing on hearing, vision, cardiac, and developmental complications. Prevention through preconception and childhood rubella vaccination remains the most effective strategy

clues.text: A newborn girl is admitted to the neonatal intensive care unit because of poor feeding, respiratory distress, and severe growth restriction noted since birth
The mother was 44 years old at the time of pregnancy, and prenatal screening demonstrated low AFP and unconjugated estriol levels
Echocardiography demonstrates a ventricular septal defect, and renal ultrasound reveals a horseshoe kidney
Examination of the feet demonstrates prominent rocker-bottom deformities

clues.detail: Physical examination reveals a prominent occiput, low-set ears, micrognathia, and clenched fists with overlapping fingers
Karyotype analysis demonstrates an extra copy of chromosome 18 resulting from meiotic nondisjunction

1.description: Trisomy 18 is a severe chromosomal disorder caused by an extra copy of chromosome 18, most commonly due to meiotic nondisjunction. Risk increases with advanced maternal age. Affected infants typically present with severe growth restriction, intellectual disability, congenital heart disease, micrognathia, low-set ears, prominent occiput, clenched fists with overlapping fingers, and rocker-bottom feet. Associated abnormalities include ventricular septal defects, horseshoe kidneys, and omphalocele. Prenatal screening often demonstrates decreased AFP, unconjugated estriol, and β-hCG levels. Diagnosis is confirmed by karyotyping.
1.management: Supportive and focuses on feeding, respiratory, and cardiac complications, as survival beyond the first year is uncommon.

clues.text: A newborn boy is admitted to the neonatal intensive care unit because of feeding difficulties, episodes of apnea, and multiple congenital anomalies identified shortly after birth
The mother was 42 years old at the time of pregnancy, and prenatal ultrasound demonstrated several structural abnormalities
Brain MRI demonstrates holoprosencephaly with incomplete separation of the cerebral hemispheres
Echocardiography reveals a ventricular septal defect

clues.detail: Physical examination reveals microphthalmia, cleft lip and palate, and postaxial polydactyly (not shown in this picture)
Karyotype analysis demonstrates an additional copy of chromosome 13 resulting from meiotic nondisjunction

1.description: Trisomy 13 is a severe chromosomal disorder caused by an extra copy of chromosome 13, most commonly due to meiotic nondisjunction. Risk increases with advanced maternal age. Affected infants typically present with profound developmental impairment and multiple congenital anomalies including holoprosencephaly, cleft lip and palate, microphthalmia, postaxial polydactyly, and congenital heart defects. Prenatal ultrasound often identifies major structural abnormalities. Diagnosis is confirmed by karyotyping. Holoprosencephaly is one of the most characteristic findings and results from failed forebrain division during embryogenesis.
1.management: Primarily supportive, focusing on feeding, respiratory, neurologic, and cardiac complications, as survival beyond the first year is uncommon

clues.text: A 27 year old girl presents with fever, headache, and progressive petechial rash
The patient has a significant history of recurrent bacterial infections
Physical examination reveals fever, nuchal rigidity, and scattered petechiae. No lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly is present
CBC demonstrates leukocytosis with neutrophilia. Quantitative immunoglobulin levels and lymphocyte subsets are normal
CH50 assay is markedly decreased
Genetic testing identifies a deficiency of a component that prevents formation of the membrane attack complex

1.description: Complement Component 5 Deficiency is a rare primary immunodeficiency caused by deficiency of complement component C5, an essential component of the terminal complement pathway. C5 participates in formation of the membrane attack complex (MAC), which is critical for killing certain encapsulated gram-negative bacteria. Patients classically present with recurrent invasive infections caused by Neisseria meningitidis and, less commonly, Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Laboratory findings typically reveal a markedly reduced or absent CH50 with otherwise normal immunoglobulin levels and lymphocyte counts. Diagnosis is confirmed by complement testing and genetic analysis.
1.management: Vaccination against meningococcal disease, prompt treatment of infections, and patient education regarding early recognition of invasive bacterial illness.

clues.text: A 42 year old man presents with recurrent fevers, lethargy, and weight loss
Visual examination of his mouth reveals oral thrush
There is generalized lymphadenopathy
He reports a history of unprotected sex with multiple partners
Labs show a reduced CD4+ T cell count
The causative agent is a single-stranded RNA retrovirus

1.description: HIV is a retrovirus that targets CD4+ T cells via the CD4 receptor, causing progressive immune depletion through direct cytopathic effects, chronic immune activation, and gut mucosal damage. Risk factors include condomless sex, injection drug use, MSM, and perinatal transmission. Diagnosis follows the CDC algorithm: 4th-generation Ag/Ab immunoassay → HIV-1/HIV-2 antibody differentiation assay → HIV RNA (NAT) if discordant. Acute retroviral syndrome (fever, lymphadenopathy, pharyngitis, rash) and AIDS-defining conditions (e.g., Pneumocystis pneumonia, Kaposi sarcoma, oral thrush progressing to esophageal candidiasis) at CD4 <200 cells/µL are highly characteristic.
1.management: HIV management centers on early initiation of antiretroviral therapy (ART), ideally within 7 days of diagnosis. Preferred regimens are integrase inhibitor–based (bictegravir/emtricitabine/TAF or dolutegravir-based combinations), achieving >90% viral suppression. Goals include durable viral suppression, immune restoration, reduced transmission, and opportunistic infection prophylaxis when indicated.

clues.text: A 7 year old boy presents with redness and pain around his scraped leg
Over the next few days, it becomes more swollen, red and tender
Inspection of the lesion shows a poorly demarcated erythema extending beyond the size of the original wound
The patient has an elevated WBC count
Skin culture grows gram positive cocci

1.description: Cellulitis is a bacterial infection of the skin and subcutaneous tissues, often resulting from a break in the skin. Classically presents with redness, warmth, swelling and sometimes fever and lymphadenopathy. Often caused by staphylococcus aureus or streptococcus pyogenes.
1.management: Oral cephalexin 500 mg QID for 10 days

clues.text: A 67 year old woman presents with progressive shortness of breath, dry cough and fever
Her symptoms have worsened over several months with hypoxemia on exertion
The patient has been on daily prednisone for the management of rheumatoid arthritis for 15 years
Lab studies reveal lymphopenia and elevated LDH

clues.detail: High-resolution CT demonstrates ground-glass opacities and reticular opacities with septal thickening

clues.text: Lung biopsy reveals silver stain positive cysts

1.description: Pneumocystitis Jirovecii Pneumonia is a serious fungal lung infection that almost exclusively infects immunocompromised patients. Causes of immunosuppression include HIV/Aids, Immunosuppressive medications, Chemotherapy etc.
1.management: First-Line drugs for PJP is Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX)

clues.text: A 6 year old boy presents with recurrent fever, skin abscesses, and pneumonia since early childhood
He has a history of slowly resolving bacterial infections despite antibiotic therapy
On exam, he has cervical lymphadenitis
Previous cultures of skin abscesses have grown S. Aureus, B. Cepacia and Nocardia species
Lymph node biopsy shows granulomatous inflammation
Neutrophils are present in adequate numbers, but testing demonstrates impaired oxidative burst

1.description: Chronic Granulomatous Disease is a rare inherited immunodeficiency due to defective NADPH oxidase in phagocytes. Patients with CGD are unable to convert oxygen to superoxide with NADPH oxidase, a key step in antimicrobial defense.
1.management: Management includes lifelong infection prevention with antibiotic and antifungal prophylaxis and aggressive treatment of infections

clues.text: A 4-month-old girl presents with chronic diarrhea, poor weight gain, and recurrent infections since early infancy.
Parents report multiple episodes of pneumonia, persistent oral thrush, and a severe rash after routine infections that other infants seemed to recover from easily.
Physical exam shows failure to thrive, oral candidiasis, and absent palpable lymph nodes
CBC with differential reveals marked lymphopenia

clues.detail: Absent thymic shadow noted in neonate CXR

clues.text: Absent germinal centers in lymph node follicles, very low T-cell receptor excision circles on neonatal screening

1.description: Severe combined immunodeficiency is a life-threatening primary immunodeficiency caused by defective T-cell development, often with impaired B-cell and NK-cell function depending on subtype. Affected infants develop severe recurrent bacterial, viral, fungal, and opportunistic infections early in life. Classic clues include chronic diarrhea, failure to thrive, persistent thrush, recurrent pneumonia, absent lymphoid tissue, absent thymic shadow, lymphopenia, and low TRECs on screening.
1.management: Protective isolation, avoidance of live vaccines, antimicrobial prophylaxis, immunoglobulin replacement, and urgent hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

clues.text: A 12 year old girl presents with diarrhea and a significant history of intermittent diarrhea
She has a history of upper-respiratory infections but otherwise appears healthy
Testing reveals Giardia Lamblia infection
Physical exam is normal
Labs show normal WBCs, T cell and B cell counts
Quantitative immunoglobulin testing shows markedly decreased serum IgA, with normal IgG and IgM levels.

1.description: Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency. IgA normally protects mucosal surfaces, especially in the respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts. Patients may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent sinopulmonary infections, chronic diarrhea, Giardia infection, allergies, or autoimmune disease. Serum IgA is low or absent, while IgG and IgM are usually normal.
1.management: Most patients only require observation. Use washed blood products if transfusion is needed to prevent anti-IgA antibody reactions

clues.text: A 56-year-old woman presents with gradually worsening headaches and subtle personality changes over the past year
She has no significant medical history but notes that symptoms have progressed slowly and are accompanied by occasional difficulty concentrating
Neurologic examination reveals mild contralateral leg weakness and hyperreflexia without cranial nerve abnormalities
The tumor is found to arise from arachnoid cap cells and is associated with loss of the NF2 tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 22
clues.detail: CT demonstrates a well-circumscribed, dural based extra axial mass with contrast enhancement
Surgical resection demonstrates a whorled pattern of spindle-shaped cells with numerous psammoma bodies

1.description: Meningioma is the most common primary benign intracranial tumor and arises from arachnoid cap cells of the meninges. It is more common in women and is associated with loss of the NF2 tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 22. Patients typically present with slowly progressive headaches, seizures, focal neurologic deficits, or personality changes due to mass effect. MRI classically demonstrates a dural-based extra-axial mass with homogeneous enhancement and a dural tail sign. Pathology reveals whorled spindle cells and psammoma bodies.
1.management: Management depends on symptoms and tumor size but may include observation, surgical resection, and radiotherapy for unresectable or recurrent lesions.

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents after noticing a painless lump in her left breast while showering several weeks ago
She experienced menopause at age 55, has never been pregnant, and her mother was diagnosed with breast cancer in her sixties
Breast examination reveals a firm, irregular, non-mobile mass in the upper outer quadrant with overlying skin dimpling
Routine lab studies show microcytic anemia and leukocytosis

clues.detail: Mammography demonstrates a spiculated irregular mass with associated microcalcifications

Core needle biopsy demonstrates malignant gland-forming cells infiltrating through the basement membrane within a dense desmoplastic stroma. Additionally (not pictured), immunohistochemistry demonstrates estrogen receptor positivity, progesterone receptor positivity, and HER2 negativity

1.description: Invasive Ductal Carcinoma is the most common type of breast cancer and arises from epithelial cells of the mammary ducts that invade beyond the basement membrane into surrounding breast tissue. Risk factors include increasing age, family history, BRCA mutations, prolonged estrogen exposure, obesity, and nulliparity. Patients typically present with a painless, firm, irregular breast mass, sometimes associated with skin dimpling or nipple retraction. Mammography classically demonstrates a spiculated mass with possible microcalcifications. Diagnosis is confirmed by biopsy and receptor testing (ER, PR, HER2).
1.management: Surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, endocrine therapy, and targeted therapies depending on tumor stage and receptor status, as well as Oncotype 21 Dx results.

clues.text: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of rapidly progressive abdominal distension and intermittent abdominal pain over the past two weeks
His parents report that the abdominal swelling seems to be enlarging noticeably from week to week, and he has recently developed night sweats and weight loss

CT scan demonstrates a bulky ileocecal mass with extensive abdominal lymphadenopathy

clues.detail: Lymph node biopsy demonstrates sheets of medium-sized B cells with numerous tingible-body macrophages creating a characteristic ‘starry-sky’ appearance

clues.text: Cytogenetic analysis reveals a t(8;14) translocation resulting in overexpression of the MYC oncogene

1.description: Burkitt Lymphoma is a highly aggressive B-cell lymphoma characterized by translocation and activation of the MYC oncogene, most commonly t(8;14). Three major forms exist: endemic (associated with Epstein–Barr virus and jaw tumors), sporadic (typically involving the abdomen, especially the ileocecal region), and immunodeficiency-associated disease. Patients present with rapidly enlarging masses and constitutional B symptoms. Pathology classically demonstrates a “starry-sky” appearance caused by tingible-body macrophages among proliferating lymphoma cells. Diagnosis is confirmed by biopsy and cytogenetic testing.
1.management: Highly responsive to intensive combination chemotherapy, making prompt recognition and treatment essential

clues.text: A 46-year-old woman presents with several months of irregular vaginal bleeding, including bleeding after sexual intercourse
She has a history of multiple sexual partners

Pelvic MRI demonstrates a cervical mass invading the surrounding stroma, and biopsy reveals invasive nests of atypical squamous cells with keratin pearl formation
Molecular studies demonstrate expression of E6 and E7 oncoproteins, resulting in inactivation of p53 and Rb tumor suppressor pathways

1.description: Cervical Cancer is most commonly caused by persistent infection with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV), particularly types 16 and 18. Viral E6 and E7 oncoproteins promote carcinogenesis by inactivating the p53 and Rb tumor suppressor pathways. Patients typically present with postcoital bleeding, abnormal vaginal bleeding, or pelvic pain. Risk factors include HPV infection, smoking, immunosuppression, multiple sexual partners, and inadequate screening. Pap testing may demonstrate high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions, while biopsy confirms the diagnosis. Squamous cell carcinoma is the most common histologic subtype.
1.management: Management depends on stage and includes surgical resection, chemoradiation, and HPV prevention through vaccination and routine screening.

clues.text: An 18-month-old boy is brought to clinic after his parents notice an unusual white reflection in his right eye in several family photographs
His father underwent treatment for an eye tumor during infancy, but the family is unsure of the exact diagnosis
Ophthalmologic examination reveals leukocoria and intermittent strabismus of the affected eye
Routine blood work is unremarkable, but genetic testing identifies a germline mutation involving the RB1 tumor suppressor gene

clues.detail: Axial CT shows bilateral orbits demonstrating an irregular partly calcified soft tissue mass

clues.text: The tumor developed after loss of the remaining normal RB1 allele, consistent with Knudson’s two-hit hypothesis

1.description: Retinoblastoma is the most common primary intraocular malignancy of childhood and results from biallelic inactivation of the RB1 tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 13. Hereditary cases follow an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern with high penetrance and often present with bilateral disease. Patients classically present with leukocoria and strabismus. Bloodwork is typically normal, making ophthalmologic examination and imaging critical for diagnosis. Ultrasound and MRI commonly demonstrate a retinal mass with calcifications. Histology may show Flexner–Wintersteiner rosettes.
1.management: Focal therapies, chemotherapy, radiation, or enucleation depending on disease extent. Survivors with hereditary disease are at increased risk for secondary malignancies, particularly osteosarcoma.

clues.text: A 3-year-old girl is brought to clinic after her parents notice a white pupillary reflex in her left eye and increasing clumsiness when reaching for objects

CBC and metabolic studies are normal

clues.detail: Orbital MRI demonstrates a solitary retinal mass with intratumoral calcifications confined to the left eye

clues.text: Molecular analysis of the tumor demonstrates two acquired somatic mutations affecting the RB1 gene, while testing of normal tissue is negative

1.description: Retinoblastoma is a pediatric retinal malignancy caused by two acquired somatic mutations in the RB1 tumor suppressor gene. Unlike hereditary retinoblastoma, patients do not inherit a germline mutation and typically present with unilateral disease at a slightly older age. Common symptoms include leukocoria, strabismus, and visual impairment. Bloodwork is usually normal, and diagnosis relies on ophthalmologic examination and imaging demonstrating an intraocular mass with calcifications. Genetic testing of peripheral blood does not reveal an inherited RB1 mutation.
1.management: Focal therapy, chemotherapy, laser photocoagulation, cryotherapy, or enucleation depending on disease extent. Prognosis is excellent when diagnosed early, and the risk of secondary malignancies is much lower than in hereditary retinoblastoma

clues.text: A 67-year-old man presents with a rapidly enlarging, painless neck mass that he first noticed six weeks ago

1.description: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma is the most common non-Hodgkin lymphoma in adults and is characterized by rapidly proliferating malignant B lymphocytes. Patients typically present with rapidly enlarging painless lymphadenopathy and constitutional B symptoms including fever, night sweats, and weight loss. Laboratory findings often include elevated LDH and anemia. PET-CT commonly demonstrates intensely FDG-avid lymphadenopathy, while biopsy reveals diffuse sheets of large atypical B cells that express CD20. DLBCL may arise de novo or through transformation of indolent lymphomas.
1.management: R-CHOP chemotherapy (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone), with cure achievable in many patients.

clues.text: A 62-year-old man presents with several months of progressively worsening constipation, increased straining, and a sensation of incomplete evacuation after bowel movements

Pelvic MRI demonstrates a circumferential rectal mass extending through the muscularis propria
Colonoscopic biopsy reveals invasive gland-forming adenocarcinoma

1.description: Rectal Adenocarcinoma is a malignant tumor arising from the glandular epithelium of the rectum, most commonly through the adenoma-carcinoma sequence involving mutations in APC, KRAS, and TP53. Risk factors include advanced age, smoking, obesity, inflammatory bowel disease, hereditary cancer syndromes, and diets high in processed meats. Patients typically present with rectal bleeding, altered bowel habits, tenesmus, weight loss, and iron-deficiency anemia. Laboratory findings may reveal microcytic anemia and elevated carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA). Diagnosis is confirmed by colonoscopy with biopsy, while pelvic MRI is used for local staging.
1.management: Neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by surgical resection and, when indicated, systemic chemotherapy

clues.text: A 64-year-old man presents with a three-month history of worsening cough, fatigue, and unintentional weight loss

1.description: Small Cell Lung Cancer is a highly aggressive neuroendocrine lung malignancy that is strongly associated with cigarette smoking. It typically arises centrally near the hilum and often presents with cough, weight loss, and early metastatic disease. Laboratory abnormalities frequently result from paraneoplastic syndromes, including SIADH causing hyponatremia and Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome causing proximal muscle weakness. Imaging usually demonstrates a central hilar mass with mediastinal involvement. Pathology reveals small blue cells with nuclear molding, high mitotic activity, and expression of neuroendocrine markers such as chromogranin and synaptophysin.
1.management: Disease is usually disseminated at diagnosis, treatment primarily consists of chemotherapy and radiation rather than surgery

clues.text: A 61-year-old woman presents with a persistent nonproductive cough and progressive fatigue over the past four months

Biopsy reveals malignant gland-forming cells with mucin production. Molecular testing identifies an activating EGFR mutation, allowing treatment with targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy

1.description: Lung Adenocarcinoma is the most common subtype of lung cancer and the most frequent lung cancer in never-smokers. It arises from glandular epithelial cells and is typically located in the peripheral lung. Risk factors include smoking, secondhand smoke exposure, radon, asbestos, and environmental pollutants. Patients commonly present with cough, dyspnea, weight loss, or may be asymptomatic. Laboratory findings are often nonspecific but may reveal anemia. Imaging classically demonstrates a peripheral spiculated pulmonary nodule or mass. Pathology shows gland formation and mucin production.
1.management: Molecular testing for EGFR, ALK, ROS1, KRAS, and other mutations is essential because targeted therapies can significantly improve outcomes in selected patients. Early stage tumours are treated surgically, radiation used for inoperable tumours or palliative settings, and immunotherapy can be combined with chemo.

clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents with a chronic cough, worsening shortness of breath, and intermittent streaks of blood in his sputum over the past three months

1.description: Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Lung is a smoking-associated non-small cell lung cancer that typically arises centrally within the major bronchi. Patients commonly present with cough, hemoptysis, dyspnea, weight loss, and symptoms related to airway obstruction. Laboratory findings may demonstrate hypercalcemia caused by paraneoplastic secretion of parathyroid hormone-related peptide (PTHrP). Imaging often reveals a centrally located hilar mass with cavitation. Histopathology classically demonstrates keratinization, keratin pearl formation, and intercellular bridges. Diagnosis is confirmed by biopsy and staging imaging.
1.management: Management depends on stage and may include surgical resection, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, radiation therapy, or combinations of these modalities.

clues.text: 24-year-old woman presents with a painless lump on the left side of her neck that has gradually enlarged over the past two months

PET-CT demonstrates contiguous mediastinal and cervical lymphadenopathy. Excisional lymph node biopsy reveals large binucleate cells with prominent eosinophilic nucleoli within an inflammatory background

1.description: Hodgkin Lymphoma is a malignant B-cell lymphoma characterized by Reed–Sternberg cells, large multinucleated cells that typically express CD15 and CD30. It most commonly presents in young adults with painless cervical or mediastinal lymphadenopathy and constitutional B symptoms including fever, night sweats, and weight loss. Laboratory findings may reveal anemia, eosinophilia, elevated ESR, and elevated LDH. Unlike most non-Hodgkin lymphomas, Hodgkin lymphoma typically spreads contiguously between lymph node groups. Biopsy demonstrating Reed–Sternberg cells is diagnostic.
1.management: Management depends on stage and generally includes combination chemotherapy, often with radiation therapy. Overall prognosis is excellent, particularly in early-stage disease.

clues.text: A 38-year-old woman presents with a slowly enlarging, painless plaque on her upper back that has been present for several years but has recently become more raised

MRI demonstrates a superficial soft tissue mass confined to the dermis and subcutaneous tissue. Biopsy reveals monomorphic spindle cells arranged in a storiform pattern infiltrating the surrounding fat in a honeycomb configuration
Molecular testing identifies a t(17;22) translocation resulting in a COL1A1–PDGFB fusion gene, and tumor cells stain strongly positive for CD34

1.description: Dermatofibrosarcoma Protuberans is a rare, low-grade soft tissue sarcoma that arises from the dermis and invades surrounding subcutaneous tissue. It typically presents as a slowly enlarging plaque or nodular lesion on the trunk or proximal extremities. Bloodwork is usually normal and not useful diagnostically. Histopathology classically demonstrates spindle cells arranged in a storiform pattern with infiltration of subcutaneous fat in a honeycomb configuration. Most cases harbor a t(17;22) translocation producing a COL1A1–PDGFB fusion gene and show strong CD34 positivity. Although metastasis is uncommon, local recurrence is frequent.
1.management: Wide local excision or Mohs micrographic surgery, with targeted therapy such as imatinib used in selected advanced cases

clues.text: A 23 year old woman is found difficult to arouse

1.description: Opioids activate μ-opioid receptors, causing CNS depression, respiratory depression, and miosis. The classic triad is coma, respiratory depression, and pinpoint pupils.
1.management: Airway support, oxygen/ventilation as needed, naloxone, monitor for recurrence because naloxone may wear off before the opioid.

clues.text: A patient recently started taking morphine for pain after surgery

1.description: Opioids bind μ-receptors in the GI tract, decreasing gut motility and secretions. Unlike nausea or sedation, constipation often persists.
1.management: Start bowel regimen: stimulant laxative such as senna or bisacodyl ± osmotic laxative such as polyethylene glycol. Consider peripherally acting μ-opioid antagonists if refractory.

clues.text: A 57 year old man presents with fatigue

1.description: NSAIDs and aspirin inhibit prostaglandin production, reducing gastric mucosal protection and increasing risk of ulceration and bleeding.
1.management: Stop NSAID/ASA if appropriate, stabilize ABCs, IV fluids/blood if needed, IV PPI, urgent endoscopy for significant bleeding.

clues.text: A patient with heavy alcohol use presents after suddenly stopping drinking two days ago

1.description: Chronic alcohol enhances GABA activity and suppresses NMDA activity. Abrupt cessation causes CNS hyperexcitability.
1.management: Benzodiazepines, thiamine before glucose, fluids/electrolyte correction, monitor for seizures/delirium tremens.

1.description: Androgenic alopecia is nonscarring patterned hair loss driven by genetic and androgen sensitivity.
1.management: Topical/oral minoxidil; finasteride for appropriate male patients; antiandrogens in selected patients.

1.description: Lichen planopilaris is a lymphocytic cicatricial alopecia that permanently destroys follicles.
1.management: Dermatology referral; high-potency topical/intralesional steroids; systemic anti-inflammatory therapy if active.

1.description: CCCA is a scarring alopecia that begins at the vertex and spreads outward.
1.management: Reduce traumatic hair practices; topical/intralesional steroids; anti-inflammatory therapy.

1.description: Folliculitis decalvans is a neutrophilic scarring alopecia with follicular pustules and tufting.
1.management: Dermatology referral; prolonged antibiotics/anti-inflammatory therapy.

The patches are smooth and round.

1.description: Alopecia areata is autoimmune nonscarring hair loss targeting hair follicles.
1.management: Intralesional corticosteroids for limited disease; topical/systemic options for extensive disease.

Lesions may involve the lips, mouth, genital skin, or nearby skin.

PCR or viral swab can confirm HSV-1 or HSV-2.

1.description: HSV causes recurrent painful vesicular eruptions after latency in sensory ganglia.
1.management: Oral antivirals such as acyclovir, valacyclovir, or famciclovir; suppressive therapy if frequent recurrences.

clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents with progressive swelling and discomfort of her left calf that developed over the past three days
Physical examination reveals unilateral calf swelling, warmth, erythema, and tenderness along the medial aspect of the inner thigh
She returned from a 14-hour international flight one week ago and recently completed treatment for colon cancer
D-dimer is markedly elevated, while CBC and basic metabolic panel are otherwise unremarkable

clues.detail: Compression ultrasound demonstrates a non-compressible thrombus within the left popliteal vein

1.description: Deep Vein Thrombosis is the formation of a thrombus within the deep venous system, most commonly in the lower extremities. Major risk factors are summarized by Virchow’s triad: venous stasis, endothelial injury, and hypercoagulability. Common causes include immobilization, surgery, malignancy, pregnancy, estrogen therapy, and inherited thrombophilias. Patients typically present with unilateral leg swelling, pain, warmth, and erythema. Laboratory findings often include an elevated D-dimer, although it is nonspecific. Diagnosis is confirmed with compression ultrasonography demonstrating a non-compressible vein.
1.management: Anticoagulation with agents such as direct oral anticoagulants or heparin, with thrombolysis or thrombectomy reserved for selected high-risk cases.

clues.text: A 43-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset shortness of breath and chest pain during inhalation that began while walking to work this morning
She has no significant PMHx, only taking estrogen-containing oral contraceptives
Physical examination reveals tachycardia, tachypnea, and mild hypoxemia. Lung auscultation is otherwise unremarkable
Arterial blood gas demonstrates respiratory alkalosis, and D-dimer is markedly elevated

clues.detail: CTPA demonstrates occlusion at the bifurcation of the main pulmonary artery, extending to the segmental and subsegmental branches of both pulmonary artery branches. Dilated pulmonary artery and enlarged right ventricle is seen, suggestive of secondary pulmonary arterial hypertension

clues.text: Lower-extremity ultrasonography identifies a popliteal deep vein thrombosis

1.description: Pulmonary Embolism occurs when thrombotic material, usually originating from a deep vein thrombosis of the lower extremities, embolizes to the pulmonary arterial circulation. Risk factors include recent surgery, immobilization, malignancy, pregnancy, estrogen therapy, and inherited thrombophilias. Patients classically present with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, and hypoxemia. Laboratory findings often include elevated D-dimer levels and respiratory alkalosis on arterial blood gas analysis. CT pulmonary angiography typically demonstrates an intraluminal filling defect.
1.management: Anticoagulation, while thrombolysis or thrombectomy may be required for hemodynamically unstable patients with massive pulmonary embolism.

clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with pain and redness along the inner aspect of her right leg that has progressively worsened over the past four days
She has a history of symptomatic varicose veins and recently returned from a prolonged road trip
Physical examination reveals a tender, erythematous, palpable cord running along the course of the great saphenous vein
CBC is normal, inflammatory markers are mildly elevated, and D-dimer is minimally increased
Compression ultrasonography demonstrates a thrombus confined to the superficial venous system without involvement of the deep veins
The patient is diagnosed with thrombosis of the great saphenous vein producing a painful palpable venous cord

1.description: Superficial Thrombophlebitis is characterized by thrombus formation and inflammation within a superficial vein, most commonly the great saphenous vein. Risk factors include varicose veins, recent intravenous catheterization, immobility, pregnancy, malignancy, and hypercoagulable states. Patients typically present with localized pain, erythema, warmth, and a tender palpable cord along the affected vein. Laboratory findings are often normal or show mild inflammatory marker elevation. Compression ultrasonography is used to confirm superficial venous involvement and exclude concurrent deep vein thrombosis.
1.management: Mobilization, compression therapy, NSAIDs, and anticoagulation for extensive disease or thrombi located near the deep venous system.

clues.text: A 63-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden severe pain and rapidly progressive swelling of her entire left leg over the past 12 hours
She was recently diagnosed with pancreatic cancer and has been largely bedridden following chemotherapy
Physical examination reveals a markedly swollen, tense, cyanotic leg with severe pain and diminished distal pulses
Bloodwork demonstrates markedly elevated D-dimer levels, hemoconcentration, and rising serum lactate
Duplex ultrasonography demonstrates extensive thrombosis involving the femoral, iliac, and popliteal veins with near-complete venous outflow obstruction
Progressive venous obstruction causes severe limb cyanosis and threatens arterial inflow, creating a high risk of venous gangrene and limb loss

1.description: Phlegmasia Cerulea Dolens is a rare but life-threatening complication of extensive proximal deep vein thrombosis, most commonly involving the iliofemoral venous system. Severe venous outflow obstruction causes massive limb swelling, pain, cyanosis, and elevated compartment pressures that may ultimately compromise arterial inflow. Risk factors include malignancy, hypercoagulable states, major surgery, and prolonged immobilization. Laboratory findings often include markedly elevated D-dimer levels and rising lactate in advanced disease. Duplex ultrasonography demonstrates extensive proximal venous thrombosis.
1.management: Vascular emergency and includes immediate anticoagulation, catheter-directed thrombolysis, thrombectomy, or other interventions to prevent venous gangrene, limb loss, and death.

clues.text: A 36-year-old woman presents with recurrent spontaneous nosebleeds that have gradually become more frequent since adolescence
She reports that her father and several relatives experienced similar lifelong nosebleeds and required treatment for vascular abnormalities
Physical examination reveals multiple small blanching red lesions on the lips, tongue, and fingertips
CBC demonstrates microcytic iron-deficiency anemia, while coagulation studies are normal
CT angiography identifies multiple pulmonary arteriovenous malformations producing right-to-left shunting
Genetic testing identifies a pathogenic mutation in the ENG gene, resulting in abnormal TGF-β signaling and fragile vascular connections

1.description: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia is an autosomal dominant vascular disorder caused by mutations affecting TGF-β signaling, most commonly involving the ENG or ACVRL1 genes. It is characterized by recurrent epistaxis, mucocutaneous telangiectasias, and arteriovenous malformations involving the lungs, brain, liver, and gastrointestinal tract. Patients frequently develop chronic iron-deficiency anemia from recurrent bleeding, while coagulation studies are typically normal. Pulmonary AVMs can cause hypoxemia, paradoxical emboli, and brain abscesses. Diagnosis is based on clinical criteria and genetic testing.
1.management: Iron supplementation, treatment of bleeding lesions, embolization of AVMs, and surveillance for complications in affected organ systems

clues.text: A 19-year-old woman presents with a lifelong history of easy bruising and recurrent nosebleeds. She recently sought medical attention because of increasingly heavy menstrual bleeding
She reports that her mother and maternal grandfather experienced similar bleeding symptoms but never required major medical intervention
Physical examination reveals scattered ecchymoses and mild mucosal bleeding without hemarthroses or deep tissue hematomas
CBC demonstrates mild iron-deficiency anemia. Platelet count and PT are normal, while aPTT is normal to mildly prolonged
Further testing demonstrates reduced von Willebrand factor antigen levels and decreased ristocetin cofactor activity
Administration of desmopressin results in a significant increase in circulating von Willebrand factor and improvement in bleeding symptoms

1.description: Type 1 von Willebrand Disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder and is usually inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion. It results from a partial quantitative deficiency of von Willebrand factor, impairing platelet adhesion and reducing factor VIII stability. Patients typically present with mucocutaneous bleeding, including epistaxis, easy bruising, menorrhagia, and prolonged bleeding after procedures. Laboratory findings often show normal platelet count and PT, with normal or mildly prolonged aPTT. Diagnostic testing demonstrates reduced von Willebrand factor activity and antigen levels.
1.management: Desmopressin for many patients, antifibrinolytics for mucosal bleeding, and von Willebrand factor replacement in severe cases or major surgery.

clues.text: 24-year-old woman presents with recurrent nosebleeds, easy bruising, and prolonged bleeding following a recent dental extraction
She reports similar symptoms in multiple family members across several generations, including both men and women
Physical examination demonstrates scattered ecchymoses and ongoing oozing from the extraction site, without hemarthroses or deep muscle hematomas
CBC and platelet count are normal. PT is normal, while aPTT is mildly prolonged. Von Willebrand factor antigen levels are near normal despite significantly reduced functional activity
Ristocetin cofactor activity is markedly reduced, and multimer analysis demonstrates loss of high-molecular-weight von Willebrand factor multimers
Further classification identifies a Type 2A variant, explaining the defective platelet adhesion despite relatively preserved von Willebrand factor antigen levels

1.description: Type 2 von Willebrand Disease is an inherited bleeding disorder caused by qualitative abnormalities of von Willebrand factor that impair platelet adhesion and, in some subtypes, factor VIII stabilization. Unlike Type 1 disease, von Willebrand factor levels may be normal or only mildly reduced, but functional activity is disproportionately decreased. Patients typically present with mucocutaneous bleeding, epistaxis, menorrhagia, easy bruising, and prolonged bleeding after procedures. Laboratory findings often demonstrate normal platelet counts and PT, mildly prolonged aPTT, reduced ristocetin cofactor activity, and abnormal multimer analysis.
1.management: Management depends on subtype and may include desmopressin, antifibrinolytics, or von Willebrand factor concentrates for significant bleeding or surgical procedures.

clues.text: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with painful swelling of his right knee that developed after a minor fall during recess
His maternal uncle experienced recurrent bleeding episodes and required frequent transfusions during childhood
Physical examination reveals a warm, swollen knee with decreased range of motion. Several large ecchymoses are present on the extremities
CBC, platelet count, and PT are normal, while aPTT is markedly prolonged
Mixing studies correct the prolonged aPTT, and factor assays demonstrate severely reduced factor VIII activity
Genetic testing identifies a mutation in the F8 gene, and treatment with recombinant factor VIII rapidly controls the bleeding episode

1.description: Hemophilia A is an X-linked recessive bleeding disorder caused by deficiency of coagulation factor VIII (Factor IX for Hemophilia B), resulting in impaired intrinsic pathway function. Patients typically present with recurrent hemarthroses, deep muscle hematomas, prolonged bleeding after trauma or surgery, and spontaneous bleeding in severe cases. Laboratory findings classically demonstrate a markedly prolonged aPTT with normal platelet count and PT. Mixing studies correct the aPTT, and factor assays confirm reduced factor VIII activity. Diagnosis is established through coagulation testing and genetic analysis of the F8 gene (F9 for Hemophilia B).
1.management: Factor VIII (or IX for B) replacement therapy, bleeding prophylaxis in severe disease, and avoidance of medications that increase bleeding risk.

clues.text: A 32-year-old woman is transferred to the intensive care unit after developing severe postpartum hemorrhage following a complicated delivery
She developed placental abruption shortly before delivery and rapidly became hypotensive despite aggressive resuscitation
Physical examination reveals diffuse oozing from intravenous catheter sites, mucosal bleeding, and extensive ecchymoses
CBC demonstrates thrombocytopenia. Coagulation studies reveal prolonged PT and aPTT, low fibrinogen, and markedly elevated D-dimer levels

clues.detail: Peripheral blood smear demonstrates numerous schistocytes consistent with microangiopathic hemolytic anemia caused by widespread fibrin deposition within the microvasculature

clues.text: The patient develops acute kidney injury and respiratory failure due to diffuse microthrombi formation while continuing to experience severe bleeding

1.description: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation is a life-threatening disorder characterized by widespread activation of the coagulation cascade, resulting in diffuse microvascular thrombosis and consumption of platelets and clotting factors. Common triggers include sepsis, placental abruption, trauma, malignancy, and severe transfusion reactions. Patients often present with simultaneous thrombosis and bleeding, including oozing from venipuncture sites, mucosal hemorrhage, and organ dysfunction. Laboratory findings classically include thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT and aPTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, and schistocytes on peripheral smear.
1.management: Management focuses on treating the underlying cause, supportive transfusion therapy when indicated, and close monitoring for life-threatening bleeding and organ failure. Platelets are not typically given.

clues.text: A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe shortness of breath and pleuritic chest pain that began shortly after a motor vehicle collision
He becomes increasingly anxious and dyspneic while awaiting further assessment, and his oxygen requirements continue to rise
Physical examination reveals hypotension, tachycardia, distended neck veins, and absent breath sounds over the right hemithorax
Arterial blood gas demonstrates hypoxemia and respiratory alkalosis

clues.detail: Portable chest radiography demonstrates a large right-sided pneumothorax with marked mediastinal shift toward the left

clues.text: Immediate needle decompression results in rapid improvement in blood pressure and oxygenation, followed by definitive chest tube placement

1.description: Tension Pneumothorax occurs when air enters the pleural space through a one-way valve mechanism, causing progressively increasing intrathoracic pressure. This pressure collapses the affected lung, shifts the mediastinum, and impairs venous return to the heart, resulting in obstructive shock. Patients typically present with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hypotension, tachycardia, jugular venous distension, and absent breath sounds on the affected side. Arterial blood gas often demonstrates hypoxemia. Diagnosis is primarily clinical and treatment should not be delayed for imaging.
1.management: Immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube thoracostomy to prevent cardiovascular collapse and death.

clues.text: A 35-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He complains of severe chest pain and increasing shortness of breath
He was restrained during the collision but sustained significant blunt trauma to the right side of his chest
Physical examination reveals tachycardia, hypotension, decreased breath sounds over the right hemithorax, and dullness to percussion
CBC demonstrates a falling hemoglobin level, and serum lactate is elevated

clues.detail: Portable chest radiography demonstrates increased density to the right hemithorax and well-defined homogenous density laterally. There is midline shift to the left. Multiple bilateral rib fractures are seen including fractures posteriorly and laterally on the right. Nasogastric tube and ETT are appropriately positioned. Blunting of the costophrenic angle
